title: Ordering, Single Active Consumer, and Partitioning · Go Semantics Case: Version Sequence Defense
summary: A Go 1.26 service team must scope order, partition by stable key, detect versions and gaps, and measure failover under bounded concurrency; reviewers reject hidden goroutine ownership, unbounded work, stale channel state, and claims that stop at a nil client error. The learner predicts values, ownership transitions, and stop paths before seeing a model. Planned artifact: Add and verify the version sequence defense increment in a keyed order and failover state machine in Go. Learners progress th…
objectives: Defend state with entity version, sequence, monotonic transition, deduplication, gap detection, and reconciliation rather than transport order alone; in Go, retain ownership, stop-path, and changed-case evidence.

Use single active consumer when one active delivery stream is required and account for failover, prefetch, recovery, and throughput limits; in Go, retain ownership, stop-path, and changed-case evidence.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: RabbitMQ and Go Predict Version Sequence Defense · Theory Version Sequence Defense
steps.instruction:
theory case — A theory Go messaging team handles case 9485 during canary Go consumer decoding old and new envelopes. Build deterministic Go evidence to enforce keyed sequence progression and single-active failover limits; keep credentials and connection URLs out of output; then fail the canary and prove older Go consumers remain compatible. Browser code uses pure values and state transitions only. amqp091-go, AMQP sockets, RabbitMQ, TLS, partitions, restarts, Docker, databases, race and leak checks, load, upgrades, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates.

Before reading, predict which action preserves the Version Sequence Defense contract for the supplied data.
